ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for bun nem nuong; mapped to the broader noodle dish class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle-based dish — composite mixed dish with meat and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Vietnamese bun nem nuong; A0CKP (composite mixed dish, noodle-based) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Vietnamese noodle dish.

main_ingredients: nuoc cham dipping sauce (fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, chilli)
allergens: gluten (possible — soy sauce in sausage marinade)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of bun nem nuong weighs approximately 450 g and contains dry rice vermicelli, several pieces of grilled pork sausage, fresh herbs, vegetables, peanuts, and a side of nuoc cham dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — component-weighted composite estimate for Vietnamese grilled pork sausage with rice vermicelli, fresh vegetables, peanuts, and nuoc cham sauce; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table (2020) for regional ingredient validation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for bun nem nuong as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by component-weighted aggregation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: cooked rice vermicelli (FDC ID 168917, Rice noodles, cooked), pork sausage grilled (FDC ID 167903, Pork, fresh, ground, cooked, pan-broiled, used as closest analogue for grilled pork sausage), raw mixed salad vegetables (FDC ID 169248), peanuts dry-roasted (FDC ID 172430), and fish sauce (FDC ID 173267). FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table (2020) was used to validate regional ingredient profiles for Vietnamese preparations. The input grabfood record values (confidence 0.7) were consistent with the USDA-derived composite estimate and are retained as a secondary cross-check only; grabfood is a forbidden primary source and is not used as primary evidence. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is the sole primary evidence basis.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632. Rice vermicelli / rice noodles (cooked) GI ~53 used as analogous food for the dominant carbohydrate source in bun nem nuong.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bun nem nuong as a composite dish. GI of 53 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed International Tables of GI and GL Values (Atkinson et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2021) for cooked rice vermicelli / rice noodles, which is the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. The protein from grilled pork sausage and fat from peanuts and sausage will further attenuate the postprandial glucose response in the composite dish, making GI 53 a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving calculated as: (53 × 13.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 4.5 serving-units per 450 g bowl) / 100 = approximately 31.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 267 mg per 100 g; a 450 g bowl delivers approximately 1,200 mg sodium, primarily from fish sauce in nuoc cham and salt in the pork sausage marinade.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request nuoc cham dipping sauce on the side and use it sparingly, limit the quantity of grilled pork sausage, and increase the proportion of fresh vegetables and herbs to keep the per-meal sodium contribution below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 13.3 g per 100 g from refined rice vermicelli (GI ~53); a 450 g bowl delivers approximately 60 g available carbohydrate, which requires careful portion management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should control portion size to half a standard bowl, increase the proportion of fresh vegetables and herbs, include peanuts to blunt the glycaemic response, and request reduced-sugar nuoc cham to limit postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.2 g per 100 g and sodium 267 mg per 100 g from processed pork sausage and fish sauce; cholesterol 27 mg per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit grilled pork sausage portions, request sauce on the side to reduce sodium, and increase the proportion of fresh vegetables, herbs, and peanuts, which contribute unsaturated fats and fibre that support a more favourable lipid profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Grilled pork sausage is a processed red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways relevant to RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute grilled pork sausage with grilled chicken breast or firm tofu, retain peanuts and fresh herbs for their anti-inflammatory micronutrient contribution, and increase the proportion of vegetables to maximise antioxidant intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bún nem nướng is a popular Vietnamese noodle dish consisting of dry rice vermicelli (bún) served at room temperature with grilled pork sausage (nem nướng), fresh lettuce, mint, coriander, bean sprouts, cucumber, shredded carrot, crushed peanuts, and a nuoc cham dipping sauce made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, and chilli. The dish is assembled rather than cooked as a single unit: the vermicelli is boiled and drained, the pork sausage is grilled over charcoal or a gas flame until caramelised and slightly charred, and the fresh components are arranged alongside. The flavour profile is a balance of savoury, smoky, tangy, and mildly sweet, with contrasting textures from the soft noodles, juicy sausage, crunchy peanuts, and crisp raw vegetables. Nutritionally, a 450 g bowl provides approximately 650 kcal, 35 g protein, 30 g fat, and 60 g carbohydrate. The glycaemic index of the dish is approximately 53 (low), anchored by the rice vermicelli, with the protein and fat from pork sausage and peanuts attenuating the postprandial glucose response. Sodium is a clinical concern at approximately 1,200 mg per bowl, primarily from fish sauce and the sausage marinade. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with peanut or fish allergies. It is widely available in Vietnamese restaurants and food courts across Singapore and Southeast Asia, and is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for bun nem nuong as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by component-weighted aggregation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked rice vermicelli, grilled pork sausage, fresh vegetables, peanuts, and fish-sauce-based dipping sauce, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA (2020) for regional ingredient validation. The input grabfood values (confidence 0.7) were consistent with the USDA-derived composite estimate and were retained as a secondary cross-check only. Medium confidence reflects the absence of a direct approved-source measurement for this specific composite dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a component-weighted composite using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: cooked rice vermicelli (FDC ID 168917), pork ground cooked pan-broiled as closest grilled pork sausage analogue (FDC ID 167903), raw mixed salad vegetables (FDC ID 169248), dry-roasted peanuts (FDC ID 172430), and fish sauce (FDC ID 173267). FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table (2020) was used to cross-validate regional ingredient profiles. Grabfood is a forbidden primary source and was used only as a secondary cross-check; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is the sole primary evidence basis. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite Vietnamese dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the FAO/INFOODS SEA table as of 2026. 3. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (Am J Clin Nutr 2021) for cooked rice vermicelli, the dominant carbohydrate source; the composite dish GI is likely lower due to protein and fat co-ingestion. 4. Halal status is unconfirmed; pork is a primary ingredient and the dish is not halal. 5. Gluten-free status is unconfirmed; soy sauce may be used in the pork sausage marinade. 6. Potassium estimated at 200 mg per 100 g from fresh vegetable components using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy vegetable entries; this is an approximation. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.5) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for grilled pork and dry-roasted peanuts; these are approximations.
